moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with this quiet, flexible move that controls the key central squares e5 and d4 without immediately committing pawns. Nf3 is the hallmark of the King's Indian Attack system—it prepares our fianchetto setup on the kingside while keeping maximum flexibility for how we'll structure our center. This move has been a favorite of players like Kasparov and Karpov because it gives us a solid, strategic game where we can outmaneuver our opponent rather than force immediate tactics.

Black stakes an immediate claim in the center, which is the natural response to our flexible opening. Black wants to control d4 and prevent us from easily establishing a strong pawn there, but this central advance also commits the d-pawn and can sometimes become a target later. We'll need to decide whether to challenge this pawn directly or use our fianchetto setup to pressure it from the side.

Now we reveal our fianchetto plan on the kingside, preparing to develop our bishop to g2 where it will exert long-range pressure along the long diagonal toward d5 and beyond. The move also safely tucks our king away on the kingside and prepares to reroute our knight from f3 to d2 or c3 when convenient. This double fianchetto setup (we'll play c4 next to fianchetto on the queenside) gives us a subtle grip on the center and control of key light squares.

Black mirrors our fianchetto setup, which is a sensible way to counter our strategy and maintain flexibility in the center. By fianchettoing on both wings, Black mirrors our setup, but this can sometimes lead to games where we have a small spatial advantage because we moved first. The stage is now set for us to continue with c4, establishing a classic double fianchetto position where piece placement and long-diagonal control will decide the game.
